item.title: Depts, Jica target effective public investment projects
item.description: THE effective enforcement of public investment management (Pim) projects is crucial for turning public funds into productive assets and to promote economic growth, says National Planning and Implementation Department Deputy Secretary Roger Kara. He said that strengthening public investment programmes and Pim institutions could close up two-thirds of the public investment efficiency gap.
item.content:encoded:
THE effective enforcement of public investment management (Pim) projects is crucial for turning public funds into productive assets and to promote economic growth, says National Planning and Implementation Department Deputy Secretary Roger Kara.

He said that strengthening public investment programmes and Pim institutions could close up two-thirds of the public investment efficiency gap.

This was highlighted during the second joint coordination committee meeting yesterday in Port Moresby with officials from National Planning, Transport department, Agriculture, and the Japan International Cooperation Agency (Jica).

The Pim is supported by Jica to strengthen the efficiency of public investment through enhanced project appraisal, monitoring and sector co-ordination.

A reports show that PIP forms a major component of the national budget as shown in this year’s allocation of over K10 billion to capital investment – a five per cent increase from last year.

“We have basically slackened off in our responsibilities, but are working on improving with Jica with us,” Kara said.

“They have been with us over the past four years and together we are working on aligning agencies to strengthen planning and appraisal and enhance budgeting and financial control.

“We also have the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank that have seen the need for the transformation to the PIP systems.

“Their involvement is to see what we have in place and assist with setting the legislation or regulation for the PIP.”

Jica chief representative Sh’irouzu Kenichi said the agency would ensure that they set guidelines to develop effective project plans and budgets.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Foundation supports early learning
item.description: BUK bilong Pikinini (BbP) marked World Earth Day yesterday at its Pari Library Learning Centre (LLC). There to celebrate the special day was the Sir Brian Bell Foundation (SBBF) chief executive officer Bronwyn Wright and chairman of the Pari United Church, Henao Kevau.
item.content:encoded:
BUK bilong Pikinini (BbP) marked World Earth Day yesterday at its Pari Library Learning Centre (LLC).

There to celebrate the special day was the Sir Brian Bell Foundation (SBBF) chief executive officer Bronwyn Wright and chairman of the Pari United Church, Henao Kevau.

The event also marked the announcement of SBBF as the new donor supporting the Pari LLC for this year.

Students and teachers took part in a range of fun and practical activities with the SBBF support squad, learning about the importance of caring for the environment, proper waste disposal, and the responsible use of resources in their daily lives.

As part of the programme, students also read about the role of a botanist and participated in colouring activities with the support of the SBBF team and BbP teachers, helping them connect environmental learning with real-world careers and creative expression.

The Pari LLC currently has 60 students enrolled, all of whom benefit from BbP’s literacy and learning programmes in a safe and supportive environment.

SBBF has been a platinum sponsor of BbP since 2019 and continues to be a key partner in supporting early childhood education in Papua New Guinea.

With the addition of Pari LLC, SBBF now supports seven of BbP’s LLCs, BbP’s inclusive education programme, and the publishing of the When I Grow Up readers.

The foundation also contributes in-kind support, with annual support valued at more than K600,000.

Wright, who is also chairwoman of the BbP Board, said: “The foundation is incredibly proud to continue our partnership and sponsorship of BbP, with the addition of Pari LLC. This sponsorship reflects our shared commitment to and belief in the impact of BbP’s work and the importance of early childhood learning opportunities in PNG.

“We look forward to continuing to build on the strong foundation we’ve created together.”

BbP remains committed to supporting children across PNG through its library learning centres and programmes, helping to build strong foundations for lifelong learning.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: DWU opens K1.24 million projects for Wewak campus
item.description: THE Divine Word University (DWU) St Benedict Wewak campus, has opened two projects worth K1.24 million –  the nursing simulation laboratory extension and the male student recreational hall. The projects were funded by the Department of Higher Education, Research, Science and Technology (Dherst) under the Government’s Public Investment Programme.
item.content:encoded:
THE Divine Word University (DWU) St Benedict Wewak campus, has opened two projects worth K1.24 million –  the nursing simulation laboratory extension and the male student recreational hall.

The projects were funded by the Department of Higher Education, Research, Science and Technology (Dherst) under the Government’s Public Investment Programme.

Department of National Planning and Monitoring secretary Koney Samuel urged students to use the new facilities responsibly as “the equipment and training dummies represent a significant investment”.

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird stressed the urgent need for more health professionals.

Bird commended DWU’s investment in modern teaching and learning infrastructure, particularly in health sciences, and pledged continued support for the university’s role in serving East Sepik and West Sepik.

DWU president Professor Fr Philip Gibbs described the facilities as long-term investments in the people and the community.

He said that the nursing simulation laboratory would bolster healthcare education by giving students confidence and practical skills.

DWU vice-president planning and development Julie Ruene said the nursing simulation laboratory extension  cost K421,448 while the hall cost K821,487.

Ruene thanked the Government and GoldBell Construction for delivering the projects on time and within budget.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Police save woman from being attacked
item.description: SWIFT action by police in Enga saved a woman accused of sorcery at Porgera from being attacked, according to provincial police commander Chief Superintendent, Steven Harris. The woman was accused of practising sorcery after a young man died from allegedly consuming alcohol continuously for two days.
item.content:encoded:
SWIFT action by police in Enga saved a woman accused of sorcery at Porgera from being attacked, according to provincial police commander Chief Superintendent, Steven Harris.

The woman was accused of practising sorcery after a young man died from allegedly consuming alcohol continuously for two days.

Harris said fortunately police officers arrived soon after being alerted of the danger the elderly woman was facing.

“Police have established strong leads, and the criminal investigation division officers are identifying those responsible while police investigation is continuing,” Harris said in a statement.

“Police reiterate that their role is to protect lives and keep people safe.

“Sorcery accusation-related violence is a horrendous act that targets the most vulnerable in our communities. It is firmly rejected by elders and leaders in Enga.”

Police alleged that members of the community living at the Suyan camp Gate in Porgera accused the elderly woman of practising sorcery following the death of the young man which sparked off violence.

Police also alleged that during the incident, a few other women were named, placing their lives at risk. 

Police, supported by the PNG Defence Force and Mobile Squad 11 members, also rescued a second woman from imminent harm.

She was taken into protective custody and is safe.

He said that no allegation or belief justified violence, and such actions constituted a criminal offence and would be dealt with by the law.

Harris urged the community to remain calm and not to take matters into their own hands.

He said that if they were at risk, or aware of someone who could be targeted by criminals, they should contact the police immediately so that action could be taken to ensure their safety.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Gun for hire
item.description: FORMER Wallabies captain and proud Papua New Guinean Will Genia is open to working with the PNG Chiefs in any capacity as he plots his next move after nearly 10 years in Japan. After retiring last year, Genia will also conclude his role as skills coach with the Kintetsu Liners at the end of the Japanese season.
item.content:encoded:
FORMER Wallabies captain and proud Papua New Guinean Will Genia is open to working with the PNG Chiefs in any capacity as he plots his next move after nearly 10 years in Japan.

After retiring last year, Genia will also conclude his role as skills coach with the Kintetsu Liners at the end of the Japanese season.

The 38-year-old is returning home to Brisbane and, while keen to pursue a coaching career, said he would relish helping the PNG Chiefs ahead of their 2028 entry.

A dual citizen, Genia’s father Kilroy served as a PNG cabinet minister, while Genia remains an ambassador for the Kokoda Track Foundation. His daughter attends school in PNG, where he retains deep ties and hopes to spend much of his post-playing life.

“If the Chiefs needed me for anything, for sure I’d love to get involved,” Genia told AAP from Japan. “I’d love to be in a mentoring space, talent-scouting, or just part of what they’re trying to build.”

He acknowledged the challenges of establishing pathways and infrastructure, pointing to Moana Pasifika’s removal from Super Rugby Pacific after five seasons as a cautionary tale.

Genia is now seeking his next opportunity after helping guide Kintetsu to the top of Japan’s division two alongside former Wallabies teammate Quade Cooper.

“I’ve really enjoyed coaching and learning about the game from a different perspective,” he said.

“I want to stay in coaching, build on my experience, and help grow the game.” – AAP

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: 15 at LFA attend first-ever referee course
item.description: FIFTEEN referees are midway through the Lae Football Association’s (LFA) Basic Referee Course, which began on Monday in the Morobean capital. Lead instructor Jacob Mathew explained that the five-day programme is one of the first tangible steps in realising new LFA president Ronald Ram’s vision for the game.
item.content:encoded:
FIFTEEN referees are midway through the Lae Football Association’s (LFA) Basic Referee Course, which began on Monday in the Morobean capital.

Lead instructor Jacob Mathew explained that the five-day programme is one of the first tangible steps in realising new LFA president Ronald Ram’s vision for the game.

“This is the first time that LFA has run a referee course to train new referees in the province,” Mathew said, noting that participants had also travelled in from other districts to attend.

Daily sessions cover all 17 Laws of Soccer and their practical application on the field.

“This course covers the fundamentals of refereeing and the key laws of the game, from Law 1 to Law 17,” Mathew added.

“It will help them run a football match as a referee, assistant referee or fourth official.

“From this training, we hope to equip participants with the necessary skills so that they can return to their districts and apply what they have learnt.”

The programme is funded by LFA, with certificates to be awarded at a closing ceremony tomorrow.

LFA match coordinator Alex Toki said referee education is central to the new president’s mandate to return to the fundamentals.

He noted that officiating development had been neglected in past years, but the current leadership is prioritising referee training from the ground up.

Mathew is delivering the technical programme, supported by Olivia Waninara, who serves as both LFA assessor and course coordinator, overseeing assessments and logistics. Newman Kaingta completes the officiating team as lead assistant, providing on-field and administrative support.

For LFA, this training marks the first step in building a newly certified group of referees in the province, with further development pathways expected to be announced in the coming months.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Building life skills for Buimo juveniles
item.description: THE Australia-sponsored inclusive rugby union programme juveniles at Morobe’s Buimo prison outside Lae is making an impact in this year’s competition, teaching respect, self-guarding and inclusion while building life skills alongside rugby skills, an official has said. Juvenile officer Sgt Carmel Chandorl said this is the first initiative of its kind for Scrum PNG to involve juveniles in a rugby u
item.content:encoded:
THE Australia-sponsored inclusive rugby union programme juveniles at Morobe’s Buimo prison outside Lae is making an impact in this year’s competition, teaching respect, self-guarding and inclusion while building life skills alongside rugby skills, an official has said.

Juvenile officer Sgt Carmel Chandorl said this is the first initiative of its kind for Scrum PNG to involve juveniles in a rugby union programme in Lae.

“The competition has not started yet; it’s only a training season for the juvenile boys. It’s the first of its kind to be involved in such activity, and I am overwhelmed,” Chandorl said.

The programme focuses on respect, self-protection, and inclusion, while also developing rugby skills. It is funded by Australia through Team Up, backed by the Australian consulate-general in Lae, and delivered in partnership with Scrum PNG and Oceania Rugby.

“We have coaches from Scrum PNG Rugby Union come in every Tuesday to train the juvenile boys as part of rehabilitation, developing both skills and mindset,” she said.

She said the juveniles first undergo classroom-based theory sessions before moving to field training.

A competition is expected to be organised soon, involving teams from outside schools or juvenile boys from Erap Boys Town.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Stage set for DEM Cup Men in League round
item.description: THE Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League (PNGRFL) is preparing for a defining moment in its 2026 season as Round 3 takes on added significance through the amplified “Men in League” round. This year’s theme places mental health, discipline, and character at the centre of the game, reinforcing the idea that strength is measured not only by physical ability but also by resilience, composure, and le
item.content:encoded:
THE Papua New Guinea Rugby Football League (PNGRFL) is preparing for a defining moment in its 2026 season as Round 3 takes on added significance through the amplified “Men in League” round.

This year’s theme places mental health, discipline, and character at the centre of the game, reinforcing the idea that strength is measured not only by physical ability but also by resilience, composure, and leadership.

Partnering with the Digicel Foundation’s Men of Honour programme, the initiative celebrates men across Papua New Guinea who embody integrity, courage, and compassion. These are individuals who build peace, strengthen families, and uplift communities through their actions.

By aligning rugby league with this message, PNGRFL is sending a powerful reminder that sport is a platform for shaping behaviour and instilling values that extend far beyond the field.

PNGRFL chief executive officer Stanley Hondina said: “Rugby league has always played a powerful role in our communities. As a game, it is a platform that shapes behaviour, discipline, and leadership.

“The Men of Honour theme is about reminding players that true strength is shown in how they respond to pressure and how they carry themselves every day,” he said.

Hondina stressed that respect, self-control, and teamwork are qualities that not only improve performance but also contribute to stronger communities.

The round follows on from the PNG Hunters’ “Anti-Violence” initiative last week, carrying forward the message: “Not every thought deserves a reaction”. In a fast-paced and emotional sport, players are constantly tested. The ability to pause, remain focused, and act with purpose is what separates reaction from leadership.

PNGRFL’s pathways & performance elite competition manager Peter Bari highlighted the importance of mental wellbeing in building strong teams.

“Mental health is a key part of performance and personal growth. Players deal with different pressures every day, and it is important that they feel supported and connected within their teams. Creating safe environments allows players to speak up and stay focused,” Bari explained.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Papua New Guinea’s healthcare is failing the people
item.description: OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane’s recent scathing criticism of the Government’s K156 billion spending spree over the last seven years can not be ignored. The Chuave MP’s criticism arose from the droves of people that lined up in the early hours of the morning for a chance to board a Chinese navy medical ship that docked in Port Moresby last week to offer free healthcare services.
item.content:encoded:
OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane’s recent scathing criticism of the Government’s K156 billion spending spree over the last seven years can not be ignored.

The Chuave MP’s criticism arose from the droves of people that lined up in the early hours of the morning for a chance to board a Chinese navy medical ship that docked in Port Moresby last week to offer free healthcare services.

“We can see a sad picture of mothers carrying infants, the elderly, and the chronically ill queuing up to receive healthcare from a Chinese medical ship. This is the result of debt-driven fiscal paralysis,” Nomane charged.

“Broken hospitals, empty medicine shelves and underpaid health workers are what we can see all throughout the country even after K156 billion has been spent since 2019 by the Government.”

According to Nomane, the Government has appropriated and spent this huge amount through the seven national budgets passed by Parliament since 2019.

That means that approximately K16.1 billion was spent in 2019, K17.6 billion in 2020, K19.6 billion in 2021, K23.4 billion in 2022, K24.6 billion in 2023, K27.3 billion in 2024, and K28.4 billion last year.

These are not figures Nomane plucked out of the air. They are the budget figures for each year named.

The Government has yet to respond to this claims by the Opposition and it is hard to see how it can because the Prime Minister himself has also expressed disappointment in the performance of his government across these years.

We do echo the Opposition Leader’s sentiments as regards the state of affairs with the health sector at the moment.

Health Minister Elias Kapavore told Parliament on March 18 that the country’s increasing population continues to put a strain on the health system, which has one health worker for every 1,000 persons.

“This is well below the World Health Organisation’s standard of a minimum of four healthcare workers for every 1,000 people,” he said.

The high population growth, at an average annual growth rate of 2.7 per cent is creating a demand for services that far outstrips the country’s capacity and availability of resources for services.

It is a cruel reality while 87 per cent of the people live in rural areas, only 34 per cent of the workforce serves there.

“This imbalance continues to deny rural communities fair and equitable access to healthcare as it cannot be delivered without people.”

He said that the Government, among its other initiatives to improve the country’s healthcare system, was committed to addressing the issue of the workforce shortage.

“Of the 32,064 established positions in the health sector, 11,135 remain unfilled, highlighting the urgent need for stronger recruitment, deployment, training and retention strategies,” Kapavore said.

“A 2024 analysis projected that by 2032, PNG will need at least 44,061 health workers to meet the increased demand for primary care services of our growing population.”

Recruitment is currently underway to recruit 7,000 to fill mont of these vacancies, particularly for rural and underserved areas.

We agree with the minister that sustainable investment in improving infrastructure, workforce, medical supply systems and public health programmes is crucial for national development.

And that means not only allocating massive amounts into the sector such as the K3.21 billion allocated in 2026 but by following funding with implementation, programme management and programme reviews and reporting.

Only such efforts can produce the results required.

The reality on the ground, such as last week’s long lines to the Chinese floating hospital ship, does not indicate that the government’s efforts has touched the basic health care needs of the majority of our people.

Proper healthcare is still plagued by the many challenges that include inadequate infrastructure, lack of human resources, insufficient funding and more.

These challenges, along with other socio-economic factors, are then exacerbated by diseases such as malaria, tuberculosis, HIV/AIDS, which are still prevalent in many parts of the country.

The people must know how the K156 billion has translated to tangible development for the nation and the health sector is a very good one to begin with.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Partnership is new era of security
item.description: THE certification of the Pukpuk Treaty is a significant milestone for Papua New Guinea and our bilateral relationship with Australia. This historic defence pact, now one step closer to entering into force, represents a strong commitment to protecting our shared sovereignty, security interests, and regional stability in an increasingly uncertain Pacific environment.
item.content:encoded:
THE certification of the Pukpuk Treaty is a significant milestone for Papua New Guinea and our bilateral relationship with Australia.

This historic defence pact, now one step closer to entering into force, represents a strong commitment to protecting our shared sovereignty, security interests, and regional stability in an increasingly uncertain Pacific environment.

As PNG’s first formal alliance with any country and Australia’s first in 74 years, the Pukpuk Treaty carries great weight.

It will enhance our defence capabilities, improve interoperability, and open new opportunities for our personnel while reinforcing PNG’s sovereignty as clearly stated in the agreement.

This important deal must be maintained by our leaders properly to protect our security and reputation.

Strong implementation, transparent reforms within the PNG Defence Force, and unwavering commitment to the treaty’s principles will ensure it delivers real benefits for our nation and future generations.

Let’s support responsible leadership that safeguards PNG’s interests while building this vital partnership.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Implement public service reforms
item.description: RECENT reforms and amendments to the Public Service Management Act (1995) are both timely and necessary. After 50 years as a nation, we have learned much about how public service delivery functions – its strengths, weaknesses, and impact from national to sub-national levels.
item.content:encoded:
RECENT reforms and amendments to the Public Service Management Act (1995) are both timely and necessary.

After 50 years as a nation, we have learned much about how public service delivery functions – its strengths, weaknesses, and impact from national to sub-national levels.

These reforms should address fundamental issues that hinder efficient and effective service delivery, while updating provisions of the Act and its policy framework where necessary.

The public service machinery must operate independently and be competitive in employment conditions to retain the country’s best talent.

This is essential for implementing government policies and delivering services effectively.

Recruitment, performance appraisal, remuneration, transfers, retirement, and leave processes must be transparent, merit-based, and free from political influence.

When these systems are credible and competitive, they will attract capable individuals and strengthen institutional performance.

The public service is often described as a “sleeping giant”.

Through practical reforms and legislative changes, it can be revitalised to fulfil its core purpose – serving the people.

Improving conditions and career pathways can also help curb the growing trend of skilled citizens leaving for better opportunities abroad.

 If left unaddressed, this brain drain will undermine national development, especially after investing limited resources in training these individuals.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Too many victims
item.description: TOO many Papua New Guineans are losing their savings to “crypto” and fast-money schemes promising unrealistic returns. Offers like turning K30,000 into K270,000 overnight are not genuine investments, but bait.
item.content:encoded:
TOO many Papua New Guineans are losing their savings to “crypto” and fast-money schemes promising unrealistic returns.

Offers like turning K30,000 into K270,000 overnight are not genuine investments, but bait.

The Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) has warned it does not license any cryptocurrency platforms or Virtual Asset Service Providers, meaning such schemes operate without oversight or protection.

These operations often rely on new investors’ money to pay earlier participants and eventually collapse, leaving many with heavy losses.

BPNG Governor Elizabeth Genia has urged the public to remain cautious and avoid unregulated schemes.

Experts stress that real wealth building takes time through savings, small businesses and education, not quick returns.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rust never sleeps: Addressing financial sustainability challenge
item.description: The decision to allocate another A$550 million (about K1.7 billion) in Official Development Assistance (ODA) grants to the Australian Infrastructure Financing Facility for the Pacific (AIFFP) was announced by Australian government in January with little fanfare and has received only limited attention. This announcement was significant, however, because it confirms the change in the AIFFP’s charact
item.content:encoded:
The decision to allocate another A$550 million (about K1.7 billion) in Official Development Assistance (ODA) grants to the Australian Infrastructure Financing Facility for the Pacific (AIFFP) was announced by Australian government in January with little fanfare and has received only limited attention.

This announcement was significant, however, because it confirms the change in the AIFFP’s character from primarily a non-concessional lending facility to one that has become increasingly reliant on more expensive (for Australia) – and scarcer – grant capital.

Since its establishment in 2019, the AIFFP’s loan-to-grant ratio has declined by more than two thirds, from a peak of 6:1 (A$3 billion (about K9.3 billion) in loans, A$500 million (about K1.5 billion) in grants) under the Coalition in 2022 to 1.9:1 (A$3 billion in loans, A$1.55 billion (about K4.8 billion) in grants) under Labor in 2026 (Figure 1).

This change in the composition of the AIFFP’s funding illustrates two underlying shifts.

The first is the long overdue recognition that a heavy reliance on non-concessional lending was never going to be a viable infrastructure financing strategy in most Pacific Island countries.

This was the case even before the regional economic and fiscal shocks that accompanied the Coronavirus (Covid-19) pandemic.

China has learned this lesson too and aid competition in the Pacific has changed.

China decreased its focus on large-scale, sovereign infrastructure lending to the Pacific almost a decade ago, before the AIFFP was established, as it has in Africa and other regions.

The second, related shift is that, despite its origins in geopolitical competition, AIFFP is looking more and more like the rest of Australia’s development programme in the Pacific – that is, a collection of mainly small to medium-sized, grant-funded projects.

The majority of the AIFFP’s projects are now exclusively grant funded and its lending portfolio remains heavily concentrated (77 per cent by value) in just one country, Papua New Guinea.

With few exceptions, AIFFP projects now also must conform to the spending and performance targets – around climate change, gender equality and disability equity – that apply to the rest of the ODA programme.

In 2022-23, the Labor government passed up the opportunity to pursue ambitious reform of the AIFFP – for example, by establishing a dedicated Development Finance Institution through which Australia could cross-subsidise the provision of concessional finance to the Pacific via non-concessional development lending and investments in higher income countries – as part of Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade’s (Dfat) development finance review.

The combined result of these two shifts is that the AIFFP’s additional funding will come from within the government’s existing ODA envelope.

The Labor government, so far at least, has “locked-in” a 2.5 per cent annual nominal increase (currently, less than the rate of inflation) to this envelope from 2026-27 to 2036-37.

Based on the AIFFP’s current grant spending rate, the A$550 million allocation equates to around four to five years’ worth of these nominal increases.

The shift to an increased emphasis on grants means that AIFFP finance will be more attractive to Pacific governments and that the demand for projects will be higher.

But this increased demand will be accompanied by a critical, high probability risk – that is, unless measures to ensure that the recurrent costs of operating and maintaining completed projects are implemented, Australian financed infrastructure investments may be neglected, may degrade and, as a result, may fail to deliver on their intended development benefits.

There is a long and sorry history of donors and governments in the Pacific, as well as in other regions, focusing on building new roads, ports and energy facilities without adequate thought given to the costs of operations and maintenance, with predictable results.

So, what are the options for meeting the AIFFP’s financial sustainability challenge? I set out several potential options below.

These options are not exhaustive. Nor are they mutually exclusive.

None is perfect and each comes with trade-offs. But they are canvassed here in the spirit of debate and discussion.

The first option would be to use AIFFP finance to reward those Pacific borrowers that have a strong record when it comes to provisioning for operations and maintenance costs.

This performance-based approach has been advocated by experts from the Lowy Institute who argue that it would be “a way of channelling investments to where they will be most effective and incentivising partner governments to undertake reforms that would improve the benefit and sustainability of AIFFP investments”.

Of course, a performance-based approach presumes an existing pipeline of high-quality projects across the Pacific that would generate the necessary financial returns to cover sustainment costs and/or that the incentive of AIFFP funding itself would be sufficient to drive policy reform. It is also blind to geopolitical considerations.

A second, conditionality-based option would be to link partner commitments to sustainment of AIFFP-funded and other infrastructure to the provision of Australian debt relief and grant-funded budget support.

On debt relief, PNG is now repaying over A$200 million (about K622 million) per year on the five non-concessional general budget support loans that Australia has provided since Covid-19.

Australia could offer to relieve some of this debt on the condition that the foregone repayments are directed toward agreed operations and maintenance costs of AIFFP-financed projects, as well as wider asset management reforms.

In relation to grant-funded budget support, the provision of such support to countries such as Fiji and Nauru could also be more directly linked to helping co-finance the sustainment costs of infrastructure assets that have been mutually designated as high priority.

These kinds of conditionalities would involve sensitive and complex bilateral negotiations.

In the case of linking sustainment funding to budget support, this would also need to be carefully calibrated to Pacific states’ individual fiscal circumstances.

item.title: Phillie fine-tuning leadership qualities
item.description: WHEN Phillie Julai Marai traveled to Hawaii in 2022 for the United States Young Pacific Leaders Conference, she had just graduated from university and was beginning her career. “It was my first year working. I was looking for opportunities to learn, grow and understand how I could contribute more effectively to my community.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Phillie Julai Marai traveled to Hawaii in 2022 for the United States Young Pacific Leaders Conference, she had just graduated from university and was beginning her career.

“It was my first year working. I was looking for opportunities to learn, grow and understand how I could contribute more effectively to my community.”

Phillie, 30, from Kokopo in East New Britain, returned home with a broader regional perspective, a strengthened network and a renewed commitment to serve her community

She was one of four Papua New Guineans selected to attend the conference – a leadership initiative sponsored by the US States Department for emerging leaders from the Pacific to discuss education, leadership development and community engagement.

Phillie had just graduated from Divine Word University in Madang with a Bachelor in International Relations degree before joining Tribal Foundation, a PNG-based non-governmental organisation.

“The experience really opened my mindset. Being with young leaders from the Pacific helped me understand what others were doing and what I could take back home to improve my work.”

It was the second overseas event she attended. She had earlier, while still at university, attended a governance and human rights workshop in Fiji.

Her interest was on education. She had even started a small private school in her home province.

“I was really drawn to education. I wanted to connect with educators and leaders who were investing in learning and see how we could support each other.”

Her regional networking reinforced the importance of collaboration beyond national borders.

“The Pacific is diverse, but many of our challenges are shared. That realization stayed with me.”

After returning home, Phillie ensured that she passed on what she learnt at the conference to young people during organised workshops.

She also applied for a small grant through the Young Pacific Leaders programme. With the grant, she established a community library at her school in East New Britain. The facility now serves students and community members.

“That was something tangible I could give back. It was important to make sure the opportunity created something sustainable.”

She is the country director for Senisim PNG, a leadership development programme under the Tribal Foundation.

We work with people from all walks of life who want better leadership and positive change. I ensure those opportunities reach as many people as possible.”

Senisim meaning “change” in Tok Pisin, focuses on promoting values-based leadership and strengthening ethical decision-making in communities and organisations.

Her role includes overseeing programme implementation, building partnerships and expanding leadership training opportunities nationwide.

“We work with people from all walks of life who want better leadership and positive change. I ensure those opportunities reach as many people as possible.”

Phillie also serves as chief executive officer of the Pacific Australia Youth Association, a regional organisation supporting youth-led initiatives in the Pacific.

“The YPL network became my professional network. Those connections created bridges for young people and open doors for regional collaboration.”

What she learned at the Young Pacific Leaders Conference continues to shape her career, not only as a leader but also as a mentor for the next generation.

It helps emerging leaders from Pacific Island countries by strengthening their leadership capacity, fostering regional cooperation and promoting people-to-people ties across the Pacific.

Through conferences, professional exchanges and alumni engagement, the programme encourages young leaders to address challenges and contribute to positive change in their communities.

Phillie encourages young professionals who already hold leadership roles to consider applying for the Young Pacific Leaders Conference.

“There are individuals who already have influence. With the right support and exposure, they can come back and make a meaningful impact on their communities.”

Her message to young people is simple: “Leadership starts with you. Change begins when you are willing to change your own behaviour, values and mindset. That’s how transformation happens.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG elections: The time to worry was then
item.description: There are irregularities, allegations, omissions, bad decisions and tragedies. The problems are so bad that, for a brief moment, the country’s political elites take notice, organise a review, launch the review and then – with the occasional exception – appear to lose all interest.
item.content:encoded:
There are irregularities, allegations, omissions, bad decisions and tragedies.

The problems are so bad that, for a brief moment, the country’s political elites take notice, organise a review, launch the review and then – with the occasional exception – appear to lose all interest.

Donors also get involved, get thwarted, get distracted (did someone say China?) and then hope, optimistically, that today’s small projects will eventually deliver improvements.

Then the next election arrives – with a bang – and problems aplenty.

Part of the answer will be familiar to anyone who has put off anything ever: time.

Too much of it. PNG’s elections occur at five-yearly intervals.

That’s long enough for urgent problems to feel like tomorrow’s problems.

The trouble is that, if you want future elections to be better, you need to start planning today.

Building a new, accurate roll, in particular, takes years.

Electoral reform ought to start in the immediate aftermath of electoral failure.

Another part of the answer is that miracle cures – usually biometric voting or electronic counting – are very tempting, particularly when touted by foreign companies with slick sales pitches.

Although technology may be tempting, it’s no substitute for the dreary work required to run elections well.

Other than making the electoral process more complex, biometric voting won’t help if the roll is a historical artefact.

And, although it’s a slow process, thanks to the presence of scrutineers, ballot counting is actually pretty good in PNG.

Good enough that the last thing anyone should want to do is introduce a complex and opaque new technology to replace it.

The clientelist nature of Papua New Guinea’s politics also contributes to the problem.

Clientelism leads to MPs focusing more on patronage, rather than national issues.

General elections are, by their very nature, a national issue and so they receive too little attention.

This structural lack of political will means that the sustained domestic effort required to improve electoral quality is always lacking, all the more so because poorly run elections actually help some MPs when it comes time to contest their seats.

Then there’s donors – or to be more specific – donor.

In PNG, most donor countries aren’t large enough to tackle major challenges such as elections.

In 2023, according to Lowy Institute Pacific Aid Map data, it gave more money to PNG than all other donors combined.

But despite the fact that it spends something in the vicinity of A$700 million (about K2.2 million) in aid a year in Papua New Guinea, and despite the importance of elections, it never seems to engage comprehensively.

Don’t get me wrong: individually there are some genuine experts involved. (Full disclosure: I currently have a minor role as part of a team providing Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade with some advice on elections globally, but my role is minor, and the expertise I’m talking about here isn’t mine.)

And Australia also provides very valuable logistical support close to the time of the election.

But it’s one thing to have experts and help at the last minute.

It’s another thing to devote substantial attention and money over time.

It’s true that Australia, as an external actor, is constrained by the extent to which Papua New Guinea’s government requests assistance, but it still has some agency.

Australian involvement will never be a panacea. But enduring, fully focused attention from Australia would nudge things in the right direction.

Too much time, wishful thinking about technical quick fixes, uninterested politicians, inadequately determined donors – mix all these ingredients together and you have an explanation as to why we’re here in 2026 with PNG’s next election scheduled for next year and so little has been achieved.
